sound_type: chatter + twig gnaw sounds -- rapid tooth-clacking chatter as warning/territorial signal, plus functional twig gnaw sounds during midden construction, E=3, rocky outcrop habitat
ecosystem: Rocky outcrop. Cliff faces, talus slopes, boulder fields, rock crevices. Uses rock overhangs and crevices for midden placement. Returns to rocky terrain.
season: year-round. Active all year. Nocturnal. Does not hibernate but may be less active in deep winter.

species_pair.pair_type: Second consecutive Cricetidae (83-84). Deer Mouse (83, ultrasonic, E=2, varied habitat) followed by Bushy-tailed Woodrat (84, chatter+gnaw, E=3, rocky outcrop). From the invisible abundant to the industrious builder.

coordinates.description: Reference for rocky outcrop habitat in the Cascade Range, Washington. Talus slopes and cliff faces in montane environments.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND CONSECUTIVE CRICETIDAE (83-84). Cricetidae streak at two. Twenty-third mammalian entry. Twenty-first mammalian species. Eleventh Rodentia. Second Cricetidae. NEW GENUS Neotoma -- woodrats (pack rats).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Neotoma from Greek neos ('new') + toma ('cutting'), referring to the sharp cutting incisors. cinerea from Latin 'ash-colored,' describing the gray pelage. Described by Ord (1815).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Neotoma -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Woodrats (pack rats). Second Cricetidae genus after Peromyscus (83).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- eleventh Rodentia in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twenty-third mammalian entry. Twenty-first mammalian species.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cricetidae's SECOND entry at degree 84. Second consecutive (83-84).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Chatter: approximately 2,000-8,000 Hz. Twig gnaw: broadband, approximately 500-5,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Chatter: approximately 3,000-6,000 Hz. Gnaw: broadband with peak energy in 1,000-3,000 Hz range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. CHATTER + TWIG GNAW SOUNDS. Tooth-clacking chatter (warning) and functional gnaw/construction sounds.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Chatter: 0.5-3 seconds per bout. Gnaw: continuous during construction, variable duration.
song_metrics.note_structure: Chatter: rapid series of tooth clicks. Mechanical, rhythmic. Gnaw: irregular scraping and snapping sounds.
song_metrics.note_types: Chatter (tooth-clacking warning), twig gnaw (construction), squeak (distress), soft contact calls.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Chatter: rapid burst of clicks. Gnaw: sustained irregular construction sounds. Both functional rather than vocal.
song_metrics.timbre: Chatter: mechanical, clicking, percussive. Like teeth chattering. Gnaw: woody, scraping, snapping. The sound of twigs being manipulated and broken.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Chatter is moderately loud -- audible at close range. Twig gnaw audible in quiet nocturnal environments. Comparable to Beaver chewing (62, E=4) but softer and smaller in scale.
song_metrics.tempo: Chatter: rapid (4-8 clicks per second). Gnaw: irregular, work-paced.
song_metrics.context: CHATTER: warning signal when disturbed, territorial defense. GNAW: functional construction sounds during midden building and maintenance. Not vocal communication but activity noise.
song_metrics.key_property: CHATTER + TWIG GNAW -- the 85th acoustic element. Tooth-clacking chatter plus functional construction gnaw sounds. E=3. Rocky outcrop. The sound of the pack rat at work. Comparable to Beaver chewing (62) but smaller scale.

physical_description.overall: Large woodrat (pack rat). 200-600 g (7-21 oz). 28-46 cm total length including tail. Bushy tail (the defining feature). Large ears, large dark eyes, gray-brown fur. The largest North American woodrat species.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 84: (1) BUSHY TAIL -- the tail is densely furred and bushy, unlike the scaly tails of most rats and mice. The defining field mark. (2) LARGE EARS -- prominent, rounded. (3) GRAY-BROWN FUR -- soft, ash-colored pelage. (4) WHITE BELLY -- clean white underside. (5) LARGE BODY -- much larger than the Deer Mouse (83).
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Soft, dense, gray-brown to cinnamon-brown. Ash-colored (cinerea). BELLY: White to cream. TAIL: BUSHY -- densely furred, squirrel-like. The key diagnostic feature. Unlike the bicolored tail of the Deer Mouse.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Large, powerful incisors adapted for gnawing bark and twigs. Neotoma = 'new cutting' -- the name refers to these cutting teeth.
physical_description.field_id: Large rat with a distinctly BUSHY tail (not scaly). Gray-brown fur, white belly, large ears. Most often detected by finding middens -- large stick nests in rock crevices. Nocturnal. The bushy tail distinguishes it from all other rats and mice.

nodes.title: Second Consecutive Cricetidae (83-84)
nodes.description: Deer Mouse (83) + Bushy-tailed Woodrat (84). Cricetidae streak at two. After the Sciuridae octet (75-82). From invisible abundant to industrious builder.

nodes.title: Chatter + Twig Gnaw -- 85th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Tooth-clacking chatter (warning) plus functional twig gnaw sounds (construction). E=3. Mechanical and functional sounds. Comparable to Beaver chewing (62) at smaller scale.

nodes.title: Midden Builder -- Thousands of Years Old
nodes.description: Elaborate stick nests (middens). Some thousands of years old. Paleontological archives. Preserved plant material and crystallized urine (amberat). Time capsules of ancient ecology.

nodes.title: Rocky Outcrop Habitat -- Returns to Rock
nodes.description: Cliff faces, talus slopes, boulder fields. Returns to rocky terrain. Shared with Golden-mantled Ground Squirrel (79). Two rocky-outcrop species in six degrees.

nodes.title: Gnaw Parallel -- Beaver Chewing (62)
nodes.description: Both produce functional gnaw sounds. Beaver chewing trees (62, E=4) and Woodrat gnawing twigs (84, E=3). Both rodent infrastructure builders. Different scales, same principle.

nodes.title: American Marten Predator (65)
nodes.description: Marten preys on Bushy-tailed Woodrats. Marten's prey list grows: Douglas Squirrel (80), Flying Squirrel (82), Woodrat (84). Cross-family predator.

nodes.title: The Damien Jurado Late Parallel
nodes.description: Both builders over time. Both accumulators. Both quiet. Both work in solitude. Jurado accumulates songs, Woodrat accumulates sticks. Songs and sticks.

nodes.title: Twenty-Fourth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-four consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. CSV reliability continues.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE CRICETIDAE (83-84). Deer Mouse is ultrasonic (E=2), Woodrat is audible chatter+gnaw (E=3). Varied habitat (83) to rocky outcrop (84). The invisible abundant followed by the industrious builder.

GNAW PARALLEL. Both produce functional gnaw sounds -- Beaver chewing trees (62, E=4) and Woodrat gnawing twigs (84, E=3). Both rodent infrastructure builders. Beaver builds dams; Woodrat builds middens. Different scales, same principle.

RODENT INFRASTRUCTURE BUILDER. Both Castoridae (Beaver) and Cricetidae (Woodrat) build structures that modify or preserve habitat. Beaver dams create ponds. Woodrat middens preserve ecological history. Both accumulate materials.

ROCKY OUTCROP SHARED. Both inhabit rocky terrain -- talus, boulder fields, rock crevices. Two rocky-outcrop species in six degrees (79, 84). Different families (Sciuridae vs Cricetidae) on the same terrain.
PREDATOR. The American Marten preys on Bushy-tailed Woodrats. Marten's prey list in the engine: Douglas Squirrel (80), Flying Squirrel (82), Woodrat (84). Cross-family predator.
